game_name: Romance of the Three Kingdoms II

design_philosophy.core_loop: Select scenario, choose warlord(s) to control, manage provinces and officers through diplomacy and conquest, expand territory until controlling all of China
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Balancing territorial expansion against officer loyalty, reputation management, and rival warlord opposition across multiple fronts
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Commanding historical warlords from Romance of the Three Kingdoms novel, unifying ancient China through strategic conquest and political maneuvering
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Game commits to simulation of 'chaos' of second-century China rather than linear historical progression; six branching scenarios allow players to replay history from different starting positions with fundamentally different power distributions; reputation system creates emergent diplomatic consequences

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Multiple scenario selection provides difficulty variation; custom character creation allows player-controlled parameter tuning; adviser system with Intelligence stat determines prediction accuracy, offering information-based difficulty scaling

progression.upgrade_system: Character stat increases through treasures and special items; officer stat growth (Intelligence, War Ability, Charm each range 10-100) impacts task success rates
progression.unlock_mechanism: Six historical scenarios unlock different initial territorial configurations and warlord allegiances; 41 provinces and 200+ unique characters available across scenarios; custom character insertion into uncontrolled territories
replayability_hooks: Six distinct historical scenarios with different starting power dynamics
200+ unique characters with individual stat distributions
Custom character creation and insertion mechanics
Reputation system affecting officer loyalty creates variable outcomes

world_building_techniques: Historical scenario selection (AD 189-220) frames player actions within Romance of the Three Kingdoms narrative context
Six warlord archetypes (Dong Zhuo, Liu Bei, Cao Cao, etc.) carry literary associations without explicit narrative constraints
Provinces and territories evoke historical geography (Luoyang, Jing Province)
200+ named officers imply faction relationships and historical context

faction_indicators: Warlord allegiances tied to historical factions (Wei, Wu, Shu emergence by AD 220)
Officer loyalty affected by reputation system—loyalty becomes social currency
Custom character creation enables player-faction identification
Territory control visualizes faction power on 41-province map
danger_signals: Reputation system creates cascading loyalty consequences—unpopular decisions destabilize officer base
Multi-front territorial expansion creates resource strain and vulnerability to betrayal
Officer stat requirements (100 Intelligence for guaranteed adviser accuracy) create information asymmetry
environmental_storytelling: Province control distribution reflects current power balance between warlords
Historical scenario selection establishes narrative context (empire fragmentation, power consolidation)
Treasures and special items as loot progression suggests plunder economy
Officer roster composition indicates faction identity and military capability
